title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In 1782, Captain Roger Umpleby built a house on a headland off the coast of Loch Cardip. By 1922, his descendant Arthur Umpleby had transformed the property into the Captain's Flag Hotel, complete with a concrete causeway, tennis courts, and a private bay. In the summer of 1922, Hercule Poirot is a guest at the hotel, relaxing on the beach. Other guests include the Gardners, Miss Bustle, Colonel Barron, Major Barry, and the vicar, Stephen Lane.

The atmosphere of the seaside resort is marked by discussions of social habits and the presence of evil. While some guests believe the tranquil location is safe, Poirot argues that evil exists everywhere, even under the sun. The arrival of Elena Marshall, a former stage actress with a magnetic and commanding presence, draws intense scrutiny. Many guests, including the vicar and Miss Bustle, view her as a menace or the embodiment of evil. Her husband, Kenneth Marshall, appears calm and indifferent to her arrival. Rosamund Darnley, a beautiful business owner and childhood friend of Kenneth, reveals to Poirot that Kenneth is a good but foolish man who repeatedly marries problematic women. She identifies Elena as a "gold digger" who has been involved in various scandals and is currently targeting Patrick Redfern, a man devoted to his wife, Christine Redfern.

Tensions rise as Elena’s presence disrupts the lives of those around her. Kenneth’s teenage daughter, Linda Marshall, harbors a deep hatred for her stepmother. Meanwhile, Christine Redfern confronts Patrick, accusing him of being infatuated with Elena. Elsewhere, Rosamund Darnley suggests to Kenneth that he should divorce Elena, but he insists on honoring his marriage commitment.

On the morning of August 25th, Elena tells Poirot she wishes to be alone on a raft. Later, while rowing near Elfin Bay, Patrick Redfern and Miss Bustle discover Elena’s body lying face down in the sand. She has been strangled. The investigation, led by Colonel Weston and various inspectors, considers several motives: Elena’s wealth, her history of attracting men, and potential blackmail. Suspects include Kenneth Marshall, who stands to inherit her estate; Patrick Redfern, who was seen with her; and various other guests. Kenneth provides an alibi involving a typewriter, which is later supported by Rosamund Darnley, though Poirot remains suspicious of her motives.

As the investigation progresses, several clues emerge: a sandwich box containing drugs found in a cave, a letter from a man named "J.N." expressing obsessive love, and remnants of a ritual in Linda Marshall's room. Poirot also notes a connection to unsolved strangulation cases involving husbands with airtight alibis.

The situation takes a dire turn when Linda Marshall is found unconscious after consuming sleeping pills, leaving a note confessing to the murder. However, Poirot realizes the truth is far more complex. He reveals that the murder was a meticulously planned operation carried out by Patrick and Christine Redfern. The pair are actually Edward and Christine Corey, serial killers who had been linked to previous unsolved strangulations.

Patrick and Christine used a sophisticated deception to commit the crime and frame others. Christine used brown sunscreen to alter her skin tone and wore a white swimsuit and a green hat to pose as a decoy corpse on the beach, tricking witnesses into seeing a "body" while the actual murder was taking place. They manipulated time by tampering with Linda’s watch and used the girl's superstitious nature to frame her, even burning evidence in her fireplace to make it appear she had performed a witchcraft ritual. Patrick killed Elena in a hidden cave (the "fairy hole") after she went there to meet him, believing he was her lover.

After Patrick attempts to strangle Poirot in a fit of rage, the truth is fully exposed. The killers' plan to use Linda as a scapegoat fails. In the aftermath, Kenneth Marshall confronts Rosamund Darnley, revealing he had suspected her of the crime due to her past. The novel concludes with the realization that the true evil was not the perceived wickedness of Elena Marshall, but the predatory nature of the Redferns.
